title: शनि स्तोत्र (दशरथकृत)
devanagariText:
॥ श्री शनि स्तोत्रम् (दशरथकृतम्) ॥

अस्य श्री शनैश्चरस्तोत्रमन्त्रस्य दशरथ ऋषिः।
शनैश्चरो देवता। त्रिष्टुप् छन्दः।
शनैश्चरप्रीत्यर्थे जपे विनियोगः॥

कोणस्थः पिङ्गलो बभ्रुः कृष्णो रौद्रोऽन्तको यमः।
सौरिः शनैश्चरो मन्दः पिप्पलादेन संस्तुतः॥१॥

एतानि दश नामानि प्रातरुत्थाय यः पठेत्।
शनैश्चरकृता पीडा न कदाचिद्भविष्यति॥२॥

दशरथ उवाच -

नमः कृष्णाय नीलाय शितिकण्ठनिभाय च।
नमः कालाग्निरूपाय कृतान्ताय च वै नमः॥३॥

नमो निर्मांसदेहाय दीर्घश्मश्रुजटाय च।
नमो विशालनेत्राय शुष्कोदर भयाकृते॥४॥

नमः पुष्कलगात्राय स्थूलरोम्णेऽथ वै नमः।
नमो दीर्घाय शुष्काय कालदंष्ट्र नमोऽस्तु ते॥५॥

नमस्ते कोटराक्षाय दुर्निरीक्ष्याय वै नमः।
नमो घोराय रौद्राय भीषणाय करालिने॥६॥

नमस्ते सर्वभक्षाय बलीमुखनमोऽस्तु ते।
सूर्यपुत्र नमस्तेऽस्तु भास्करे भयदाय च॥७॥

अधोदृष्टे नमस्तेऽस्तु संवर्तक नमोऽस्तु ते।
नमो मन्दगते तुभ्यं निस्त्रिंशाय नमोऽस्तु ते॥८॥

तपसा दग्धदेहाय नित्यं योगरताय च।
नमो नित्यं क्षुधार्ताय अतृप्ताय च वै नमः॥९॥

ज्ञानचक्षुर्नमस्तेऽस्तु काश्यपात्मज सूनवे।
तुष्टो ददासि वै राज्यं रुष्टो हरसि तत्क्षणात्॥१०॥

देवासुरमनुष्याश्च सिद्धविद्याधरोरगाः।
त्वया विलोकिताः सर्वे नाशं यान्ति समूलतः॥११॥

प्रसादं कुरु मे देव वरार्होऽहमुपागतः।
मौलौ नम्रशिरा नित्यं भक्तेष्वभयदो भव॥१२॥

॥ फलश्रुति ॥

स्तोत्रेणानेन सन्तुष्टः शनैश्चरः सुलोचनः।
वरं प्रादाद्दशरथं शनि दोषादि शान्तिकृत्॥

उत्तराषाढासम्भूतो राजा दशरथो मुदा।
पुत्रवान्धनवांश्चैव शनिकोपान्मुमोच सः॥

॥ इति श्री दशरथकृत शनि स्तोत्रं सम्पूर्णम् ॥
transliteration:
|| Shrī Śani Stotram (Daśarathakṛtam) ||

Asya Shrī Śanaiścharastotramantrasya Daśaratha ṛṣiḥ.
Śanaiścharo devatā. Triṣṭup chhandaḥ.
Śanaiścharaprītyarthe jape viniyogaḥ.

Koṇasthaḥ piṅgalo babhruḥ kṛṣṇo raudro'ntako yamaḥ.
Sauriḥ Śanaiścharo Mandaḥ Pippalādena saṁstutaḥ.||1||

Etāni daśa nāmāni prātarutthāya yaḥ paṭhet.
Śanaiścharakṛtā pīḍā na kadāchidbhaviṣyati.||2||

Daśaratha uvācha -

Namaḥ Kṛṣṇāya Nīlāya Śitikanthanibhāya cha.
Namaḥ Kālāgnirūpāya Kṛtāntāya cha vai namaḥ.||3||

Namo nirmāṁsadehāya dīrghaśmaśrujatāya cha.
Namo viśālanetrāya śuṣkodara bhayākṛte.||4||

Namaḥ puṣkalagātrāya sthūlaromṇe'tha vai namaḥ.
Namo dīrghāya śuṣkāya kāladaṁṣṭra namo'stu te.||5||

Namaste koṭarākṣāya durnirīkṣyāya vai namaḥ.
Namo ghorāya raudrāya bhīṣaṇāya karāline.||6||

Namaste sarvabhakṣāya balīmukhanamo'stu te.
Sūryaputra namaste'stu bhāskare bhayadāya cha.||7||

Adhodṛṣṭe namaste'stu saṁvartaka namo'stu te.
Namo mandagate tubhyaṁ nistriṁśāya namo'stu te.||8||

Tapasā dagdhadehāya nityaṁ yogaratāya cha.
Namo nityaṁ kṣudhārtāya atṛptāya cha vai namaḥ.||9||

Jñānachakṣurnamaste'stu kāśyapātmaja sūnave.
Tuṣṭo dadāsi vai rājyaṁ ruṣṭo harasi tatkṣaṇāt.||10||

Devāsuramanusyāścha siddhavidyādharoragāḥ.
Tvayā vilokitāḥ sarve nāśaṁ yānti samūlataḥ.||11||

Prasādaṁ kuru me deva varārho'hamupāgataḥ.
Maulau namraśirā nityaṁ bhakteṣvabhhayado bhava.||12||

|| Phalaśruti ||

Stotreṇānena santuṣṭaḥ Śanaiścharaḥ sulochanaḥ.
Varaṁ prādāddaśarathaṁ Śani doṣādi śāntikṛt.

Uttarāṣāḍhāsambhūto rājā Daśaratho mudā.
Putravāndhanavāṁśchaiva Śanikoopānmumochasaḥ.

|| Iti Shrī Daśarathakṛta Śani Stotraṁ sampūrṇam ||
hindiMeaning: इस श्री शनैश्चर स्तोत्र मंत्र के ऋषि दशरथ हैं, देवता शनैश्चर हैं, छन्द त्रिष्टुप् है, शनैश्चर की प्रसन्नता के लिए जप में विनियोग है। कोणस्थ, पिंगल, बभ्रु, कृष्ण, रौद्र, अन्तक, यम, सौरि, शनैश्चर और मन्द - ये शनि के दस नाम हैं जो पिप्पलाद मुनि ने स्तुति किए। जो प्रातः उठकर इन दस नामों का पाठ करे, उसे शनि की पीड़ा कभी नहीं होती। राजा दशरथ ने कहा - काले, नीले, शिव के समान कण्ठ वाले को नमस्कार। कालाग्नि रूप और कृतान्त (यम) को नमस्कार।
benefits: Classical tradition invokes this for removal of afflictions caused by saturn (shani sade sati, dhaiya)
Composed by King Dasharatha, father of Lord Rama
Invokes kingdom when pleased, removes when angered
Traditionally invoked for protection from accidents, chronic diseases, and sudden losses
Invokes the blessing of wealth, progeny, and prosperity
Most powerful stotra for Shani Graha Shanti
description: Among the most cherished prayers to Lord Shani Dev, Shani Stotra (by King Dasharatha) (शनि स्तोत्र (दशरथकृत)) stands as a sacred prayer. This sacred composition is sourced from Skanda Purana - Dasharathakritam and continues to inspire devotees worldwide. Devotees recite this prayer to bestow wealth, progeny, and prosperity and protect from accidents, chronic diseases, and sudden losses. It is also said to composed by King Dasharatha, father of Lord Rama. For best results, devotees chant this on Saturday (Shanivar). Comprising 12 verses, it can be completed in approximately 8 minutes, making it suitable for daily devotional practice. On this page, you can read the complete Shani Stotra (by King Dasharatha) in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.
seoDescription: Read Shani Stotra by King Dasharatha in Sanskrit with Hindi meaning. Most powerful prayer for Saturn planetary remedy and Sade Sati protection.

seoFaqs.question: What is the Shani Stotra and which version does this page contain?
seoFaqs.answer: Shani Stotra is the broad family of devotional hymns dedicated to Lord Shanaishchara, the slow-moving son of Surya and Chhaya who governs Saturn's karmic discipline. The version preserved on this page is the Dasharatha-krita Shani Stotram drawn from the Skanda Purana tradition, in which King Dasharatha of Ayodhya pleased Shani with this hymn when Saturn was about to enter Rohini nakshatra. Other widely circulated versions include the short Nilanjana Samabhasam dhyana shloka from the Vyasa Navagraha Stotra and the Shani Stotra found in the Brahma Vaivarta Purana. The Dasharatha-krita version remains the most commonly recited on Saturdays across India. (Sources: Stotra Nidhi; Hindupedia Shani; Vedadhara.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the source of the Shani Stotra in classical scripture?
seoFaqs.answer: The Dasharatha-krita Shani Stotra appears in the Skanda Purana within the Shani Mahatmya sections, with parallel references in Padma Purana and Brahma Vaivarta Purana. The dhyana shloka Nilanjana Samabhasam Ravi-putram Yamagrajam is from the Vyasa-krita Navagraha Mangala Stotram, used as the standard dhyana for any Shani worship. Compilation texts such as Brihat Stotra Ratnakara and the Navagraha Stotra Mala used by South Indian temple priests collect multiple Shani stotras. Gita Press, Stotra Nidhi and major regional publishers print the Dasharatha-krita version as the canonical Shani stotra. (Sources: Stotra Nidhi; Hindupedia; Vedadhara.)

seoFaqs.question: When is the best time to recite the Shani Stotra?
seoFaqs.answer: Saturday (Shanivar) is the universally accepted weekday for any Shani Stotra, with the Shani-hora hour after sunrise being the most auspicious window. The annual peak occasions are Shani Jayanti on Vaishakha Amavasya, Shani Amavasya (when Amavasya falls on a Saturday), and Shani Trayodashi. Devotees experiencing the seven-and-a-half-year Sade Sati transit, the two-and-a-half-year Dhaiya, Saturn retrograde or Saturn rashi-change periods are encouraged to recite the stotra daily. Other recommended times include before legal proceedings, surgeries, long journeys, or any period of unexplained delay, depression or chronic illness suggesting Saturn affliction. (Sources: Drikpanchang Shani Jayanti; Hindupedia Sade Sati; Stotra Nidhi.)

seoFaqs.question: What benefits does the Shani Stotra phalashruti promise?
seoFaqs.answer: The Skanda Purana phalashruti of the Dasharatha-krita Shani Stotra promises that the devotee gains the favour of Shani Dev, escaping the harshest effects of Sade Sati and Dhaiya. Other promised benefits include freedom from graha-pida (planetary affliction), prevention of accidents and untimely death, healing of chronic Saturn-associated illnesses like arthritis, paralysis and depression, restoration of lost wealth and employment, success in court cases, and the return of estranged family members. The phalashruti specifically notes that even one recitation on Shani Jayanti equals the merit of 108 ordinary recitations and that daily recitation accelerates the burning of past karmas, aiding eventual moksha. (Sources: Stotra Nidhi; Vedadhara; Hindupedia.)

seoFaqs.question: What offerings traditionally accompany the Shani Stotra?
seoFaqs.answer: The traditional Shanivar offerings used during Shani Stotra recitation are sesame oil (til tel) for the lamp — often a four-wicked chaturmukha lamp; black sesame seeds; urad dal; black or dark-blue cloth; mustard oil; iron items; blue Aparajita or Shami flowers; and a naivedya of til-laddu, urad khichdi, or kala chana. Devotees light the sesame-oil lamp before a Shani murti or yantra at home or at a Shani temple such as Shani Shingnapur, Tirunallar or Mandapalli, and circumambulate seven and a half times to symbolise Sade Sati. Non-ritual practices considered equally meritorious include feeding crows (Shani's vahana), serving the elderly, disabled or labouring poor on Saturdays. (Sources: Drikpanchang Shanivar; Hindupedia; Sanatan Sanstha.)

seoFaqs.question: How is the Shani Stotra different from the Shani Chalisa?
seoFaqs.answer: The Shani Stotra is a Sanskrit hymn drawn from puranic sources — most commonly the Dasharatha-krita version from the Skanda Purana — opening with the twenty-nine names of Shani and structured as classical stuti verses. The Shani Chalisa is a 40-verse devotional composition in Awadhi-Hindi modelled on the Hanuman Chalisa pattern, attributed to later Hindi devotional tradition rather than to puranic sources. The stotra is preferred by Sanskrit-trained pandits and for formal puja, while the chalisa is widely used by Hindi-speaking householders for daily devotional reading. Many devotees combine both — chalisa for daily personal devotion and the stotra during Saturday vrat or Shani Jayanti. (Sources: Hindupedia Shani; Stotra Nidhi; Drikpanchang.)

seoFaqs.question: Can Shani Stotra recitation reduce the effects of Sade Sati?
seoFaqs.answer: Classical tradition holds that no remedial recitation can eliminate the karmic content of Sade Sati, which represents the maturation of past karmas and is a necessary phase of spiritual learning. However, regular Shani Stotra recitation is believed to soften the manifestation of these karmas — converting catastrophic blows into manageable lessons, accelerating dharmic insight, and preventing the worst material setbacks. Each of the names invoked in the stotra addresses a different facet of Shani's nature, allowing the devotee to invoke his benevolent rather than punitive face. Combined with sesame-oil lamp offerings, charity to the poor on Saturdays, and ethical conduct, the recitation is one of the most respected Saturn remedies. (Sources: Hindupedia Sade Sati; Drikpanchang; Stotra Nidhi.)
